title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Relationships
meta_description: Stop second guessing yourself. Your gut feelings about relationships are real. Learn to trust your intuition and make clearer decisions.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Relationships
og_description: Stop second guessing yourself. Your gut feelings about relationships are real. Learn to trust your intuition and make clearer decisions.
body:
You Already Know What You Want. You Just Don't Trust It.

You've been there. Sitting across from someone who's perfect on paper. Or lying awake at 3 AM replaying a conversation. Or staring at your phone, knowing you should text them back, but something in your gut says "wait."

And then the voices start. The ones that sound reasonable. The ones that say "you're being dramatic" or "just give them another chance" or "you always do this, you're too picky."

Here's the thing no one tells you: that tight feeling in your chest, the one you've been told is anxiety? Sometimes it's not anxiety at all. Sometimes it's your nervous system telling you something your conscious mind hasn't caught up to yet.

I'm Kalesh, and I've spent years working with people who feel everything too much. People who walk into a room and know within seconds whether the energy is safe. People who get physical symptoms around certain people and then spend hours explaining them away. You're not broken. You're not too sensitive. You're just calibrated for a wider range of information than most people are comfortable with.

Let's talk about how to actually use that gift when it comes to relationships - without driving yourself crazy second-guessing everything.

The Difference Between Intuition and Fear

This is the first wall everyone hits. "How do I know if it's my gut or just my anxiety?"

Here's a shortcut that took me years to learn: intuition is quiet. Fear is loud.

Real intuitive hits feel like a knowing. They don't come with a story attached. You don't get a PowerPoint presentation with bullet points about why you should leave your partner. You just know. It's a settled feeling. Even if the knowing is painful, there's a weird peace underneath it.

Fear, on the other hand, comes with a narrative. It loops. It shows you worst-case scenarios. It says "but what if..." over and over. Fear needs you to stay stuck, because fear is trying to keep you safe from a threat that may or may not be real.

Try this the next time you're unsure: close your eyes and bring the decision to mind. Notice where you feel it in your body. If the feeling is tight, spinning, and located in your head - that's fear. If the feeling is a knowing in your chest or your gut, and it's still even if it's heavy - that's intuition.

Does that land?

Your Body Is Smarter Than Your Brain About People

You've been conditioned to think that good decisions come from logic. Spreadsheets. Pros and cons lists. Weighing evidence.

But your body has been tracking people your whole life. It noticed the micro-expression your partner made when you brought up your feelings. It registered the slight shift in their voice when they said "I'm fine." It felt the energy change when your friend walked into the room.

Your conscious brain might miss all of that. But your body? Your body took notes.

"The body never lies. It doesn't have a PR department. It doesn't care about being polite. It just tells you the truth, and then it's up to you whether you're ready to hear it."

Start paying attention to how you feel physically around different people. Not emotionally - physically. Does your jaw clench? Do your shoulders drop? Does your stomach relax or tighten? Do you feel more tired after spending time with them than you should?

These aren't random. They're data. Real data that your rational mind can't access because it's too busy trying to be fair and reasonable.

I'm not saying ditch logic entirely. I'm saying your body gets a vote. And right now, you probably haven't been letting it speak.

The Problem With "Following Your Gut" in Relationships

Here's where it gets tricky. You've probably been burned before by trusting your intuition. Maybe you thought you had a feeling about someone and it turned out wrong. Maybe you left a relationship because something felt off, and then you spent months wondering if you made a mistake.

This is normal. Intuition is not infallible. It's a sense, like sight or hearing. And just like your eyes can play tricks on you in low light, your intuition can get distorted when you're triggered, tired, or attached to a specific outcome.

The goal isn't to follow your intuition blindly. The goal is to learn to read it accurately. And that takes practice.

One of the best tools I've found for this is journaling. Not the "dear diary" kind - the kind where you write down what you're feeling without editing it. Especially when you're in the middle of a relationship decision. Write down what your body is saying. Write down the fears. Write down the quiet knowings. Then go back and read it a week later and see what turned out to be true.

You'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that the things you wrote down but didn't act on? Those were the ones that came true. You'll notice that the fears that came with a full story attached? Those were usually wrong.

If you want a structured way to start tracking this, I highly recommend picking up a copy of Sonia Choquette's "Trust Your Vibes" on Amazon (paid link). It's a practical workbook that walks you through exactly how to distinguish your intuitive hits from your fears, with exercises you can do in five minutes a day.

How to Ask Your Intuition a Direct Question

Most people don't know how to ask their intuition for guidance. They just sit there and wait for a feeling to hit them like a lightning bolt. That's not how it works for most of us.

Here's a method I teach my students:

First, get your body into a relaxed state. Take three deep breaths. Drop your shoulders. Unclench your jaw. You can't hear intuition when you're in fight-or-flight.

Then, ask a yes or no question. But here's the key: don't ask "should I stay with them?" That's too big. Ask something smaller. Ask "is this person safe for me to be vulnerable with?" Ask "does this relationship support my growth right now?" Ask "am I tolerating something here that I shouldn't be?"

Notice the first response that comes. Not the one your brain starts spinning on - the first one. It might be a word. It might be a physical sensation. It might be an image. The first response is usually the cleanest.

Then wait. If more information comes, great. If not, that's the answer. Trust it.

This takes practice. You're going to get it wrong sometimes. That's fine. You're learning a new language. You didn't learn to speak English in a day. Why would intuition be any different?

When Your Intuition Says "No" But Your Heart Says "Yes"

This is the hardest one. You love them. You want it to work. But something in your gut keeps saying "no."

Here's what I want you to understand: intuition doesn't always mean "leave." Sometimes it means "proceed with caution." Sometimes it means "this isn't the right time." Sometimes it means "you need to set a boundary before you go any further."

The problem is that we tend to see intuition as binary. It's either a green light or a red light. But real intuition is more like a dimmer switch. It gives you shades of information.

If you're getting a "no" but you're not ready to act on it, that's okay. Ask your intuition for more clarity. Ask "what do I need to know about this situation?" Ask "what would make this feel safer?" Ask "what am I not seeing?"

Sometimes the answer is "you need to have a hard conversation." Sometimes it's "you need to give it three months and check again." Sometimes it's "you already know, and you're just not ready to admit it yet."

Be honest with yourself about where you are. There's no shame in not being ready to act on what you know. The shame is in pretending you don't know.

For those times when you need to slow down and actually hear what your intuition is saying, I recommend "The Gifts of Imperfection" by Brené Brown on Amazon (paid link). It's not a psychic development book - it's a book about having the courage to be honest with yourself, which is the foundation of all intuitive work.

Using Intuition to Choose a Partner (Before You're In Too Deep)

Most people choose partners based on chemistry and then try to use intuition to fix problems later. That's backwards.

Your intuition is most useful before you're emotionally invested. In the first few dates, when you're still neutral enough to actually see what's in front of you.

Here's what I tell my clients: pay attention to how you feel in your body during the first three dates. Not after. During. Are you relaxed? Are you performing? Are you trying to be the version of yourself that you think they'll like? Are you laughing because you're actually having fun, or because you're nervous?

Also pay attention to how you feel after you leave them. Do you feel energized? Drained? Confused? Peaceful? The after-feeling is often more telling than the during-feeling, because your nervous system is no longer in social mode.

If you consistently feel drained, confused, or smaller after spending time with someone, that's your intuition waving a red flag. Don't ignore it just because they're attractive or successful or nice on paper.

I also want you to pay attention to how they make you feel about your own intuition. Do they respect it? Do they get defensive when you bring up a feeling? Do they try to talk you out of your own experience? A partner who dismisses your intuition is a partner who will eventually dismiss your boundaries.

If you're serious about learning to trust yourself in relationships, pick up "The Intuitive Way" by Penney Peirce on Amazon (paid link). It's one of the most practical books I've ever read on actually applying intuition to daily life, including relationships.

What to Do When Your Intuition and Your Friends Disagree

This is where it gets really uncomfortable. Your friends love you. They want what's best for you. And sometimes they're dead wrong about your relationships.

Not because they're bad friends. Because they can't feel what you feel. They're operating on the information you've given them, which is always filtered through your own confusion and fear. They don't have access to the body-level knowing that you have.

Here's a hard truth: if you keep asking other people what to do, you're outsourcing your own intuition. You're giving away your power because it's scary to trust yourself.

I'm not saying never ask for advice. I'm saying ask for advice after you've checked in with yourself. Get your own answer first. Then share it with trusted people and see if they can see any blind spots. But don't let them override what you already know.

If you find yourself constantly asking friends for relationship advice, take that as a sign that you're not trusting your own guidance. Start small. Make a decision about something minor based on a feeling. See what happens. Build that trust muscle.

One practice I love: before you talk to anyone about a relationship decision, write down what your intuition is saying. Seal it in an envelope. Then after you've talked to your friends, open it and see if you changed your mind because of new information or because you got scared.

This is how you learn to trust yourself. By watching yourself be right over and over again.

Red Flags vs. Your Own Stuff

Here's the nuance that most people miss: not every uncomfortable feeling is a red flag. Sometimes the feeling is your own unhealed stuff getting triggered.

If you have abandonment wounds, you might feel a "no" every time someone doesn't text you back fast enough. That's not intuition. That's trauma.

If you have a history of being controlled, you might feel trapped in a relationship that's actually healthy. That's not intuition either. That's your nervous system running old programs.

This is why self-awareness is so critical. You have to know your own patterns before you can trust your intuition. Otherwise you're just reacting to your past.

How do you tell the difference? Ask yourself: does this feeling have a history? Have I felt this way before in similar situations? If the answer is yes, it might be a pattern, not a present-moment intuitive hit.

But here's the thing: even if it's your own stuff, that doesn't mean you should ignore it. It means you need to heal it. A triggered nervous system can't give you clean information. You have to do the work to clear the static.

One of the best tools I've found for sorting out your own patterns is "The Body Keeps the Score" by Bessel van der Kolk on Amazon (paid link). It's heavy, but it will change how you understand your own reactions. Read it slowly.

A Simple Decision-Making Protocol for Relationships

Let me give you something you can actually use starting today. A step-by-step process for when you're stuck.

Step one: get quiet. No phone, no music, no distractions. Five minutes minimum.

Step two: bring the decision to mind. Don't try to solve it. Just hold it.

Step three: scan your body. Where do you feel it? What's the quality of the sensation? Tight? Heavy? Light? Expansive?

Step four: ask a specific yes/no question. "Is this relationship good for me right now?" "Should I have this conversation?" "Is this person being honest with me?"

Step five: notice the first response. Don't analyze it. Just notice it.

Step six: check if the response is consistent. Ask the same question three different ways. If you keep getting the same answer, trust it.

Step seven: act on it. Even if it's a small action. Action builds trust with your intuition.

That's it. It's not complicated. The hard part is doing it consistently, especially when you don't like the answer.

You Already Know

I'm going to tell you something that might piss you off, but I mean it with love: you already know what to do about every relationship decision you're facing right now. You've always known. You just haven't been willing to admit it to yourself.

The knowing is there. It's in the way your stomach drops when you think about staying. It's in the relief you feel when you imagine leaving. It's in the exhaustion that comes from pretending everything is fine.

Your intuition has been talking to you this whole time. You've just been trained to ignore it in favor of being "reasonable" or "fair" or "nice."

Stop being nice to people who make your nervous system scream. Stop being fair to people who take more than they give. Stop being reasonable when your body is telling you something isn't right.

You're not too sensitive. You're not broken. You're not making things up. You're a deeply feeling person living in a world that doesn't know what to do with you. And that's not your problem to fix.

Your only job is to learn to trust the information you're receiving. To act on it. To let it guide you toward relationships that actually feel good, not just ones that look good on paper.

Start small. Trust the little hits. See what happens. You might be surprised at how right you've been all along.

If you want more help figuring out where you're at with all of this, I've put together a short assessment that can help you see where you're blocking your own intuition. Take the intuition assessment here - it'll give you some clarity on what's getting in the way.
